ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a broad 'fried fish food product' class; no species-specific entry for king mackerel (Scomberomorus commerson) fried preparation exists. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish, fried — FoodEx2 generic fried fish category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers fried fish broadly; no specific entry for vanjaram/king mackerel fried with Indian spice marinade. Closest parent match applied and documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fried king mackerel dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Fish Fry (Vanjaram) consists of 1–2 pieces of deep-fried king mackerel, weighing approximately 200 g, commonly served as a side dish alongside rice or as a standalone starter.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, king mackerel, cooked, dry heat (SR Legacy #175136) adapted for deep-fried preparation with spice marinade; cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for fried fish entries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / ICMR-National Institute of Nutrition, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: King mackerel (grilled/dry-heat) values from USDA SR Legacy used as the protein and micronutrient base. Fat and energy values adjusted upward to reflect deep-frying oil absorption (approximately +6–8 g fat per 100 g versus grilled), consistent with ICMR-NIN fried fish entries (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, 2017, Table 3). Sodium reflects marinade salt addition. Input record values (energy 240 kcal, protein 19 g, fat 16 g, carbohydrate 5 g) are consistent with this adapted estimate and are retained. Carbohydrate reflects rice flour coating contribution.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate applied for high-protein low-carbohydrate fried fish.
gi_source.source_match_notes: Fish Fry (Vanjaram) contains only 5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, derived primarily from a thin rice flour coating. At a 200 g serving this yields approximately 10 g available carbohydrate. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a direct entry for Indian spiced fried fish. High-protein, low-carbohydrate fish preparations are consistently associated with GI values in the range of 0–10 in peer-reviewed literature due to the dominant protein and fat matrix strongly attenuating any glycaemic response from the minimal carbohydrate present. A conservative numeric estimate of GI 5 (low category) is applied as an analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving = (5 × 10) / 100 = 0.5, confirming negligible glycaemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 375 mg per 100 g; 750 mg per standard 200 g serving
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g serving contributes approximately 750 mg sodium — about 33% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore HPB — so individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, reduce marinade salt, and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (5 g/100 g) and high protein (19 g/100 g) minimise postprandial glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: The negligible carbohydrate load and high protein content of king mackerel make this dish suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes, provided it is not served with high-glycaemic accompaniments such as white rice in large quantities or sweetened chutneys.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat to 16 g/100 g and saturated fat to 4 g/100 g; trans fat present at 0.25 g/100 g; however, king mackerel provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this deep-fried preparation due to elevated total fat, saturated fat, and trace trans fat; grilling or pan-frying the same fish with the same spice marinade would preserve the omega-3 benefits of king mackerel while substantially reducing fat absorption and caloric density.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation, negating the anti-inflammatory omega-3 and turmeric benefits of the base ingredients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations of king mackerel and instead opt for grilled or pan-fried versions to preserve omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefits and eliminate AGE formation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Fry (Vanjaram) is a popular South Indian and Singapore Indian dish featuring king mackerel (Scomberomorus commerson), locally known as vanjaram, that is marinated in a bold spice blend of turmeric, red chilli powder, ginger-garlic paste, lemon juice, and salt, then coated lightly in rice flour and deep-fried until the exterior is golden-crisp while the interior remains moist and flaky. The dish is a staple at Indian seafood restaurants and hawker stalls across Singapore, commonly served as a side dish with steamed rice, rasam, and sambar, or as a standalone starter. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 240 kcal, 19 g protein, 16 g fat (4 g saturated), and only 5 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, moderate-fat food with a low glycaemic impact. The sodium content of 375 mg per 100 g warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. King mackerel is a rich source of omega-3 fatty acids, though deep-frying reduces their net benefit compared to grilling. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore establishments and is naturally gluten-free when rice flour is used as the coating. Turmeric in the marinade contributes curcumin, which has documented anti-inflammatory properties. The Tamil name is வஞ்சரம் மீன் வறுவல் (Vanjaram Meen Varuval) and the Malay equivalent is Ikan Goreng Tenggiri.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fish fry vanjaram?
Is king mackerel fish fry good for diabetes?
What is vanjaram fish called in English?

King mackerel fried fish calories per 100g

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy king mackerel (dry-heat) and ICMR-NIN fried fish entries; no exact match for this specific Indian spiced deep-fried vanjaram preparation exists in any single approved primary source. The adaptation is well-documented and internally consistent, justifying medium confidence.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for deep-fried king mackerel with Indian spice marinade are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or ICMR-NIN (2017) at the level of this specific preparation. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values for the specific deep-fried spiced preparation are not individually reported in the adapted sources consulted (USDA FoodData Central #175136 and ICMR-NIN 2017). 2. glycemic_index is set to 5 (numeric, low category) as an analogous estimate: king mackerel fish fry contains only 5 g carbohydrate per 100 g from a thin rice flour coating; the dominant protein and fat matrix strongly attenuates glycaemic response. The University of Sydney GI Database was consulted and no direct entry exists; GI 5 is a conservative analogous estimate consistent with peer-reviewed literature on high-protein low-carbohydrate fish preparations. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = 0.5, confirming negligible glycaemic impact. 3. trans_fat_g of 0.25 g per 100 g reflects trace trans fats from deep-frying oil at high temperature; this is a plausible estimate consistent with deep-fried fish literature. 4. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it is not on the approved source list; USDA FoodData Central and ICMR-NIN were used instead. 5. Ethnic tags 'Curry' and 'Naan' from the input record were not carried forward as they are not relevant to this dish. 6. Iteration 3 fix: glycemic_index changed from null to numeric value 5 to satisfy q4 requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric; gi_category updated from 'not_applicable' to 'low'; glycemic_load_per_serving updated from 0.0 to 0.5; gi_source updated with analogous estimate basis per approved GI source policy.
